1.synthesis: In Egypt, “the LORD was with Joseph, and he became a successful man” (v.2); Potiphar sees that the LORD prospers all he does and sets him over his entire house (vv.3–6). But Potiphar’s wife casts her eyes on the handsome young slave and presses him daily, “Lie with me” (v.7). Joseph refuses, framing it as betrayal of his master and, above all, of God: “How then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?” (v.9). When she seizes his garment, he flees, leaving it in her hand; scorned, she turns the evidence into a false accusation of attempted rape (vv.12–18). The integrative thread is integrity under temptation and injustice: the man whom God prospers is slandered for his very faithfulness, and obedience costs him everything.
1.historical_context: Potiphar was “captain of the guard” (v.1), a high Egyptian official, and the entrusting of a Hebrew slave with the whole household (v.4) reflects the real possibility of a capable servant’s advancement in Egyptian estates. The repeated daily pressure (v.10) and the seizing of the garment (v.12) — a second garment used against Joseph, after the robe of Genesis 37 — show the relentlessness of the temptation. The wife’s appeal to racial resentment, “he brought among us a Hebrew to laugh at us” (v.14), exploits Egyptian prejudice to make her lie credible.
1.literary_structure: The chapter is bracketed by the refrain “the LORD was with Joseph” (v.2, v.21), framing both his rise and his fall under the same divine presence. The garment left in the woman’s hand (v.12) is the structural echo of the bloodied robe (Genesis 37:31): twice a garment becomes false witness against the innocent Joseph. The deliberate contrast with the preceding Judah-and-Tamar episode is stark — Judah pursued the roadside woman; Joseph flees the seductress.
1.theological_application: Joseph models the flight from sexual sin that Scripture commands — “flee from sexual immorality” (1 Corinthians 6:18; 2 Timothy 2:22) — and locates the deepest reason for purity in reverence for God: sin against Potiphar and against the woman is finally “sin against God” (v.9; cf. Psalm 51:4). The passage teaches that faithfulness may be punished rather than rewarded in the short term, and that the believer must obey regardless of consequence, trusting God with the outcome (1 Peter 2:19–20). Integrity that costs is integrity proved.
1.christology: Joseph, the righteous servant falsely accused and condemned though innocent, foreshadows Christ, against whom false witnesses testified (Matthew 26:59–60) and who “committed no sin” yet suffered unjustly, entrusting himself “to him who judges justly” (1 Peter 2:22–23). As Joseph’s unjust descent into prison would prove the road to his exaltation over Egypt, so Christ’s unjust condemnation was the path to his exaltation as Lord (Philippians 2:8–9). The presence of the LORD with the suffering, slandered servant (v.21) is the assurance that God does not abandon his righteous ones in their affliction.

20.synthesis: Joseph is thrown into the king’s prison, “but the LORD was with Joseph and showed him steadfast love and gave him favor in the sight of the keeper of the prison” (vv.20–21). Soon the keeper entrusts everything to Joseph’s hand, “because the LORD was with him. And whatever he did, the LORD made it succeed” (vv.22–23). The integrative thread is the constancy of God in the deepest descent: in the dungeon as in the house, the same divine presence and blessing rest on Joseph, so that even the place of unjust imprisonment becomes a place of trust and stewardship — and, providentially, the very location where he will meet Pharaoh’s officials.
20.historical_context: The “king’s prison” (v.20) where state prisoners were held would prove strategically placed, for it housed Pharaoh’s own disgraced officials (Genesis 40:2–3). That Joseph, a foreign slave convicted of assaulting his master’s wife, is not executed but imprisoned may hint that Potiphar half-doubted his wife’s story. The keeper’s total reliance on Joseph (v.22) mirrors Potiphar’s earlier trust, the same God-given competence and favor following him even into chains.
20.literary_structure: These four verses are a hinge, closing the Potiphar episode and opening the prison sequence that leads to Pharaoh’s court. The fourfold repetition of “the LORD was with him / the LORD made it succeed” (v.21, v.23) hammers home the chapter’s theme with liturgical insistence. The structural rhyme between Potiphar’s house and the prison — favor, entrustment, success — shows Joseph rising again from the bottom, a pattern the narrative will complete when he rises from prison to the palace.
20.theological_application: “The LORD was with Joseph” even in prison (v.21) is the bedrock comfort of the suffering saint: God’s presence is not withdrawn in adversity but is most tangibly given there (Psalm 23:4; Isaiah 43:2). The passage teaches that faithfulness in the lowest place — diligent stewardship even in a dungeon — is itself a form of worship, and that God can prosper his servant’s work in the unlikeliest circumstances. Believers are encouraged to serve well where God has placed them, trusting that no season of unjust suffering is wasted in his hands. Joseph’s steady diligence in the dungeon also rebukes the temptation to abandon faithfulness when faithfulness has gone unrewarded; he does not grow bitter or idle but serves the prison-keeper as he had served Potiphar, leaving vindication to God. Such perseverance in obscurity is itself precious in God’s sight (Galatians 6:9).
20.christology: Joseph’s steadfast service in the place of unjust confinement prefigures Christ, who in his humiliation “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Hebrews 5:8) and whose lowest descent was the appointed path to glory. The “steadfast love” (Hebrew ḥesed) shown to Joseph in prison (v.21) is the covenant faithfulness perfectly displayed in Christ, who is Immanuel, “God with us” (Matthew 1:23), present with his people in every prison. The road from dungeon to throne traced here is the gospel shape of exaltation through suffering (1 Peter 1:11).
